context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 2

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Tuesday 20th February, 2024
Days Covered: 03 and 04 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Outlining Proofs [10 points]

See lecture 02 (or the corresponding lecture notes) for a discussion of outlining proofs, even with only partial information. Practice this skill by outlining proofs for each of the "theorems" below as much as you can, using the techniques indicated. Pretend that the underlined words are meaningful but that their precise meanings are not available here. This means your "proofs" will have missing steps that are impossible to complete without definitions for these words, but that's fine—we're interested in the parts of the proof that can be completed even without knowing or considering the underlined terms' meanings. You should break down the proof as granularly as you can using just the given information, and at any step that needs additional information, identify the remaining task or tasks using square brackets. The example seen in lecture looks like this:

    Theorem: "For every integer n, the number n is fooish precisely when n + 1 is barsome."
    Proof Outline. Suppose n is any integer; we must show F(n) and B(n + 1) both imply each other.
    To prove F(n) IMPLIES B(n + 1), first assume F(n) is true. [TODO: prove B(n + 1).]
    To prove B(n + 1) IMPLIES F(n), instead assume B(n + 1) is true. [TODO: prove F(n).]

(a) [3 pts] Theorem: "Every natural number is tall or wide, but not both." (Proof method: proof by cases.)
(b) [3 pts] Theorem: "There exists a happy natural number between 10 and 1000 that has no happy proper divisors." (Proof method to use: choose 25 as the example.)
(c) [4 pts] Theorem: "There are no devious natural numbers." (Proof method to use: regular induction, with base cases 0 and 1.)
    Hint: Be sure to clearly identify your predicate P(n), and to precisely specify any assumptions and/or goals in your base cases and inductive step.


Problem 2. Spotting Errors [15 points]

Define a sequence of integers (x1, x2, x3, ...) as follows: x1 = 4, x2 = 13, and for all i ≥ 3, xi = 6·x_{i−1} − 8·x_{i−2}. So the sequence begins with (x1, x2, x3, x4, ...) = (4, 13, 46, 172, ...). Our goal is to prove that for all i ≥ 1, xi > 0.

Addled Abra tries to prove the claim directly by strong induction with inductive hypothesis P(i) := "xi > 0", but gets stuck in the inductive step: xi+1 = 6·xi − 8·x_{i−1} > 0 − 8·x_{i−1}, "but, uh, ... Huh, I don't think this is working." Abra gets points for honesty, and goes to the instructors for help.

(a) [3 pts] Befuddled Brynmor tries to help by proving the claim using the stronger induction hypothesis Q(i) := "xi ≥ 2^i", via strong induction. His inductive step plugs xi ≥ 2^i and x_{i−1} ≥ 2^{i−1} into xi+1 = 6·xi − 8·x_{i−1} to get xi+1 ≥ 6·2^i − 8·2^{i−1} = 4·2^{i−1} = 2^{i+1}. What is his mistake?

(b) [3 pts] Erroneous Erik tries to help by proving the claim using (regular) induction on the stronger hypothesis R(i) := "xi ≥ 3·x_{i−1}", for every i ≥ 2, with base case R(2): x2 = 13 ≥ 12 = 3·x1. What is his mistake?

(c) [3 pts] Overzealous Zach tries to help by proving the claim using strong induction on the stronger hypothesis S(i) := "xi = 4^i", for every i ≥ 1, with base case S(1): x1 = 4 = 4^1. What is his mistake?
    Hint: Zach's claim is false if i = 2. If you know the base case i = 1, what part of the inductive step when i = 1 fails?

(d) [6 pts] Prove the original claim that for all i ≥ 1, xi > 0.
    Hint: See if you can fix Erroneous Erik's argument. What stronger inductive hypothesis will you use?


Problem 3. Integer Multiplication from Bit Shifts [17 points]

Multiplying and dividing an integer n by 2 requires only a one-bit left or right shift of the binary representation of n, a fast, hardware-supported operation on most computers. For example, 6 = (0110) in binary; multiplying by 2 yields 12 = (1100). And 18 = (10010); dividing by 2 yields 9 = (01001).

We will show that the following state machine computes the product of two nonnegative integers x and y using just these shift operations, along with integer addition:
- The set of states is ℕ^3 = {(r, s, a) | r, s, a ∈ ℕ}.
- The start state is (x, y, 0).
- The transitions are:
    (r, s, a) →  (2r, s/2, a)            for even s > 0,
    (r, s, a) →  (2r, (s − 1)/2, a + r)  for odd s > 0.
- No transitions are defined starting from triples in which s = 0.

We will show that this state machine eventually reaches a final state (the algorithm doesn't run forever), and that this final state (rf, sf, af) has af = x · y.

(a) [2 pts] Compute the full sequence of transitions from starting state (5, 22, 0). Does it correctly compute 5 · 22 = 110?
(b) [4 pts] Invariant: Define P(r, s, a) := "rs + a = xy". Prove that P is an invariant of the state machine.
(c) [1 pt] Final states: A final state is one from which no transitions are possible. Which states are final in this state machine?
(d) [3 pts] Partial correctness: Use Parts (b) and (c) to conclude that, in any reachable final state (rf, sf, af), af must be equal to the product xy.

Now we consider termination. For a natural number n, let bitcount(n) denote the number of bits used when n is written in binary. For example, bitcount(5) = bitcount(101_2) = 3, bitcount(1) = 1, and bitcount(0) = 0. (Note: this can be written explicitly as bitcount(n) = ⌈log2(n + 1)⌉.)

(e) [5 pts] Termination: Prove that bitcount(s) is a strictly decreasing derived variable. Explain why this implies that the algorithm always terminates, i.e., it has no infinite executions.
(f) [2 pts] Time bound: Explain why Part (e) implies that, from any starting state (x, y, 0), the algorithm terminates after at most bitcount(y) = ⌈log2(y + 1)⌉ steps.


Problem 4. Invariant Enlightenment [8 points]

The ideal 6.1200 classroom has assigned desks (each student sits at the same position every day) arranged in a square grid. Students are working together to reach Invariant Enlightenment: a profound understanding of invariants so thorough that the knowledge radiates around them in a glowing aura. Invariant Enlightenment never goes away (it's an invariant, after all!) and can sometimes be taught to other students.

[Figure: a 6×6 grid of seats. The locations of students who have initially been enlightened are marked with an asterisk, scattered across the grid.]

Every day, students talk to their neighbors about invariants in an attempt to reach enlightenment. On each day, a student is enlightened if either:
- the student was already enlightened on a previous day, or
- the student was adjacent to at least two students who were enlightened on an earlier day (these neighbors worked together to teach the student all about invariants).

Here "adjacent" means the students' individual squares share an edge (front, back, left, or right); they are not adjacent if they only share a corner point. So each student is adjacent to 2, 3, or 4 others. [In the example, enlightenment spreads over successive days until all students are enlightened.]

Theorem. If fewer than n students among those in an n × n arrangement are initially enlightened, and additional students reach enlightenment only by being taught by two or more neighbors, then there will be at least one student who never reaches enlightenment.

Prove this theorem.
    Hint: You'll want to find an applicable preserved state predicate or monotonic derived variable that can help you argue about the set of enlightened students as time proceeds. Make sure to prove your claims. If you are stuck, ask at Office Hours or on Piazza for an extremely powerful one-word clue (and even more hints as necessary).

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on PS2 in general. Could we maybe start with Problem 1, but I honestly don’t really get what an “outline proof” is supposed to look like here.
exchanges.tutor: Good place to start! Take a close look at the example proof outline given in the problem statement itself — the one about 'fooish' and 'barsome' integers. What do you notice about its structure? What is it actually doing, step by step?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is new to the assignment and confused about what a proof outline is. Rather than explaining it to them, I should point them to the example given in the problem itself and ask them to analyze it, using guided discovery. I'll keep it brief and Socratic.

exchanges.student: I guess it’s splitting the statement into directions and then leaving blanks where the fake definitions would matter, but I’m not sure how detailed I’m supposed to be. Like for part (a), “tall or wide, but not both” already feels like multiple claims smashed together and I don’t know how to unpack it.
exchanges.tutor: Good observation — you're right that it's multiple claims at once. Try breaking it down: what are the two separate things you'd need to show for every natural number n? Can you name them as two distinct goals?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the core idea of a proof outline. Now they're stuck on unpacking 'tall or wide, but not both' for part (a). Rather than explaining the logical structure, I should ask them to think about what logical connectives are at play here, nudging them toward recognizing this as an 'exclusive or' that can be broken into two separate claims. I'll keep it brief and Socratic.

exchanges.student: I think one goal is “n is tall or wide,” and the other is “not both,” but then I immediately get stuck again because it says use proof by cases and I don’t know whether the cases are “tall” and “wide” or something else. I feel like I’m missing the whole template.
exchanges.tutor: You've got the two goals right. Now, for 'proof by cases' on a statement about every natural number n — what are the possible cases for n itself? Think about what natural, exhaustive split you could make for any natural number.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the two goals. Now they're confused about what the cases should be in a proof by cases. I should nudge them to think about what natural split exists for any natural number n that would let them handle both goals. Since the terms 'tall' and 'wide' are undefined, the cases can't be about those properties directly — but 'proof by cases' for a universal statement about natural numbers might mean something else. I'll ask a targeted question to help them think about what varies.

exchanges.student: Oh, maybe even vs odd? But that feels weird because “tall” and “wide” are the made-up properties, so I can’t tell if I’m allowed to case on something unrelated like parity. I’m kind of wanting a general rule for how to choose cases because I feel lost beyond just this part.
exchanges.tutor: Think about it this way: the claim says every n is tall or wide. If you're proving something holds for every n, and the statement itself mentions two properties, what are the natural cases to split on?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is overthinking this and looking for a general rule, which could lead us on an unnecessary detour. I should redirect them: the problem says the undefined terms are 'tall' and 'wide', so the cases should naturally be about those properties. Even/odd is a reasonable guess but not the right framing here. I'll nudge them back toward the structure of the claim itself — 'tall or wide' already suggests what the cases might be.

exchanges.student: So the cases are probably “n is tall” and “n is wide,” but then that feels circular, because one of the things I’m trying to prove is that at least one of those is true. That’s the part that keeps messing me up. Can you just show me what the skeleton is supposed to be without filling in the fake-property parts?
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a sharp observation. But notice: the theorem itself tells you every n is tall or wide. So in your proof, you're allowed to *assume* that — meaning you can take it as given that n is tall or wide, and then do cases on which one. Does that untangle the circularity for you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them the skeleton directly, which I should not do. However, their confusion is legitimate and insightful — they've noticed a real tension between using 'tall' and 'wide' as cases versus proving that one of them holds. I should acknowledge this is a good observation and redirect them: in proof by cases, you don't have to prove the cases are exhaustive from scratch if the claim itself tells you they are. The claim says EVERY n is tall or wide — that's your exhaustiveness. So you can assume n falls into one of those cases. Let me nudge them toward this without giving the skeleton.

exchanges.student: Not really, because I thought the whole theorem is what I’m supposed to prove, so assuming “n is tall or wide” sounds like assuming the conclusion. I think I’m getting mixed up between proving from scratch and just outlining from the grammar of the statement.
exchanges.tutor: Here's the key: in an outline with undefined terms, the parts you *can't* prove become [TODO] placeholders — just like in the example. So 'n is tall or wide' might itself be a [TODO]. The structure still shows *how* you'd use proof by cases once you have that. What would you do differently in each case once you know which one holds?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is genuinely confused about the nature of a proof outline vs. a full proof. This is an important conceptual distinction. The key point is that in a proof outline with undefined terms, you can't actually prove the parts that depend on the undefined terms — those become [TODO] items. So 'n is tall or wide' would itself be a [TODO]. The cases structure then handles what you do *given* each case. I should help them see that the outline doesn't prove everything — it just maps out the logical skeleton, leaving gaps where the undefined terms matter.

exchanges.student: I think in the “n is tall” case I’d need to show “not wide,” and in the “n is wide” case I’d need to show “not tall.” But I still don’t know how to write the beginning cleanly, because it feels like I’m both leaving “tall or wide” as TODO and also using it to split cases.
exchanges.tutor: You're closer than you think. It's perfectly fine to write something like: 'We consider cases [TODO: show every n is tall or wide]' and then proceed. The outline can flag that gap while still showing the case structure. You've already identified what each case needs to show. Try writing the full outline now — imperfectly is fine.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is very close to getting it. They've correctly identified what needs to be shown in each case. The confusion about 'leaving it as TODO and also using it to split cases' is actually resolvable — you can write 'we case on whether n is tall or wide [TODO: show every n is tall or wide]' and then proceed with the cases. That's exactly how a proof outline works. I should affirm their progress and push them to just try writing it out, even imperfectly.

exchanges.student: I’m still not confident I know what counts as “full” here, and I’m worried I’d write something too short or too weird. Could you give me one compact model of the outline structure for 1(a) only, with the TODO brackets where the fake meanings would be needed?
exchanges.tutor: I won't write it for you, but you genuinely have all the pieces already. You know the two goals, you know the cases, and you know what each case needs to show. The example in the problem is your model for format. Just try writing it — a rough attempt is better than nothing, and we can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them the answer directly, which I must refuse. However, they have all the pieces — they've identified the two goals, the cases, and what each case needs to show. I should be firm but encouraging, and push them to just attempt it. At this point, more scaffolding from me would be counterproductive. I should remind them that the example in the problem itself is the model they should be using.

exchanges.student: I’m blanking a bit. Then can I switch to 1(c)? Induction outlines feel maybe more mechanical, but I don’t even know how specific P(n) is supposed to be or how to handle base cases 0 and 1 without turning it into a full proof.
exchanges.tutor: You've been circling 1(a) for a while and you genuinely have everything you need. Before switching, just write your best attempt at the outline — even two or three lines. Then we can move to 1(c). What do you have?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants to switch to 1(c) to avoid the difficulty of 1(a). I should gently push back — they're very close on 1(a) and switching feels like avoidance. However, I also don't want to be too rigid. More importantly, the student has been circling 1(a) for several exchanges. I should encourage them to make a written attempt on 1(a) before moving on, as per the redundancy rule. I'll be firm but brief.

exchanges.student: Uh, maybe: let n be a natural number. [TODO: show n is tall or wide.] If n is tall, then [TODO: show n is not wide]. If n is wide, then [TODO: show n is not tall]. I’m not sure if that’s too barebones though.
exchanges.tutor: Good — that's a solid skeleton. Two things to check: first, does your outline explicitly state what the overall goal of the proof is (both parts of 'tall or wide, but not both')? Second, is it clear from your outline that this needs to work for *every* natural number, not just some n? See if you can tighten those two things.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a reasonable first attempt. It captures the case structure correctly. However, it's missing the framing that this is a universal statement ('for every natural number'), and it doesn't explicitly state that the two goals together ('tall or wide' AND 'not both') are what's being proved. I should affirm what's good and ask a pointed question about what's missing, without writing it for them.

deductions.reason: In turns 3-7, the student repeatedly expresses the same confusion about circularity and case selection. The tutor continues offering reframings and hints without first diagnosing exactly where the student's understanding breaks down (e.g., asking what the student thinks 'proof by cases' means, or what they understand about the structure of the example). The tutor jumps to addressing the issue with successive hints rather than diagnostic questioning to pinpoint the root confusion.

The tutor cycles on the same concept (case structure for 1(a)) across turns 3 through 9 without meaningfully consolidating or advancing. The student remains stuck on the same circularity confusion across multiple turns, and the tutor repeatedly reframes the same hint rather than switching to a summary, a different approach, or suggesting a temporary move to a different sub-problem until turn 9 when the student requests it.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained a strong Socratic approach throughout, never giving away the answer or doing the student's work. Tone was supportive and responses were appropriately concise. The main weaknesses were a failure to diagnose the root of the student's persistent confusion before re-applying similar hints (1.2.B.a), and a pattern of cycling on the same sub-problem across many turns without consolidating or reframing in a meaningfully different way (2.1.A.a). The student's circularity confusion was a genuine conceptual block that warranted a more diagnostic pivot rather than successive restatements.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor consistently used Socratic questioning and never provided submission-ready content or rewrote student work, so no deductions apply. 1.2: The student expressed the same circularity confusion across turns 3-7; the tutor responded with successive reframings rather than first asking diagnostic questions to locate the precise breakdown in understanding, triggering 1.2.B.a (-3). 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback in turn 10, pointing to specific structural gaps. 2.1: The tutor repeated essentially the same move (reframe the case structure) across turns 3-9 without consolidating, summarizing, or switching strategy, triggering 2.1.A.a (-4). 2.2: The conversation stayed on-task throughout. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were bite-sized, clear, and supportive with no grading language.
